:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. Understanding the relative proportion of these two groups is pivotal for evaluating the effectiveness of a state's criminal justice policies.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In New Hampshire, sentences have a minimum and a maximum term. However, if a sentence has a maximum of over one year (i.e. a felony sentence), the judge must also impose a 'disciplinary period' of incarceration of 150 days per year of the minimum term that can then be reduced through credit for good conduct. For example, a three to eight year sentence would have a 450 day 'disciplinary period' added on to the minimum term. If a sentence of imprisonment is imposed, the maximum term may not exceed: life for second-degree murder, 15 years for a class A felony, seven years for a class B felony, or one year for a class A misdemeanor. Some crimes carry mandatory minimum sentences. For example, a second or subsequent conviction for the felonious use of a firearm carries a mandatory minimum sentence of three years. A conviction under the state's version of the Armed Career Criminal Act carries a minimum sentence of ten years. Prisoners may be released on parole at the expiration of the minimum terms of their sentences, plus the disciplinary period of incarceration that must be imposed during sentencing, and minus any credit for good conduct. So, for example, if an individual is given a sentence of 10 to 25 years, they must serve their 10 year minimum and, in addition, 1,500 days (or about four years, one month, and 10 days) of a disciplinary period. However, through good time credit, the inmate can pare the 14 years plus that must be served before parole release eligibility back down to 10 years. Thus, the disciplinary period and good time are designed to offset each other.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 238 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 11% of the prison population.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 89% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 89% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 35-44 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 32% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 2-4.9 years, accounting for 37% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to rape or sexual assault, accounting for 23% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::
